question: Can TwinSpires race card and odds pages be scraped from a local Python process? What are the actual page URLs for Derby weekend entries and live odds, is the content server-rendered or JS-rendered, and are there rate limits, authentication walls, or anti-bot measures that would block a simple requests/httpx scraper?

Is Equibase data accessible for a personal local project — is there a free or low-cost API, or does it require scraping? What are the actual URL patterns for Churchill Downs race entries and results, and what format does the data come back in (HTML, JSON, XML)?

Does KentuckyDerby.com expose odds and entries data in a structured format (JSON endpoint, embedded JSON-LD, or structured HTML), or is it pure opaque HTML/JS rendering that requires a headless browser to parse?

How can a scraper programmatically determine which specific race numbers constitute the Friday Pick 5 (Kentucky Oaks day) and Saturday Pick 5 (Kentucky Derby day) sequences at Churchill Downs — is this published in a structured field in any of the three named sources, or does it require hardcoding race numbers each year?

When during Derby weekend do live/current pari-mutuel odds first become available on TwinSpires or KentuckyDerby.com, how frequently do they update (seconds, minutes?), and do they require wagering account authentication to access, or are they publicly visible without login?

Where are late scratches published in structured or semi-structured form for Churchill Downs races — are they embedded in the entries data from Equibase/TwinSpires, posted as a separate endpoint or page, and how close to post time do they typically appear?

What is the right local storage mechanism for OddsSnapshot records (which accumulate across multiple refreshes throughout race day) — SQLite, append-only JSON/JSONL files, or a lightweight embedded store — given that the app must survive restarts, serve cached data on refresh failure, and display intra-day odds drift per horse?

Are there more scraper-friendly or API-accessible alternatives to TwinSpires/Equibase/KentuckyDerby.com for Churchill Downs race cards and odds — e.g., DRF, Brisnet, NYRA/AmWest, or any public racing-data APIs — that would be more reliable for a personal project with no commercial license?
